source_content: Chongqing PSB Nan'an Branch Police Bulletin, 2024-05-19
headline: Multi-panel simulation finds the police bulletin's core conclusions resilient against distortion. Diverse participants converged on consensus across all four debated issues: the fraud finding is legally sound, the sister's cyberbullying was illegal, the bulletin is factually credible, and the case should not be reduced to gender warfare.

key_reaction_patterns.description: Participants who initially rejected the 'no fraud' conclusion based on the 79.9M figure changed their views when presented with the complete financial picture (79.9M out, 46.3M back, 7.5M shared-account surplus). The most dramatic evolution occurred in young, emotionally-driven participants who updated from categorical rejection to nuanced acceptance within 4 rounds.

key_reaction_patterns.pattern: Folk morality converges with legal analysis
key_reaction_patterns.description: Participants without legal training (rural elders, small-business owners, delivery riders) consistently reached the same conclusions as legal analysts through intuitive moral reasoning: 'hiring people to curse others is wrong,' 'official numbers this specific should be real,' 'a young person dying is not a gender issue.'

key_reaction_patterns.pattern: Selective citation is the primary distortion mechanism
key_reaction_patterns.description: Across all panels, the most effective distortion tactic was presenting only the 79.9M transfer figure without the 46.3M counter-transfers. This tactic was deployed by both traffic-seeking content creators and ideological agitators, and was consistently neutralized when other participants provided complete data.

key_reaction_patterns.pattern: Mental health emerges as the unaddressed consensus concern
key_reaction_patterns.description: Regardless of starting position, all four panels organically converged on concern for youth mental health support. Participants agreed that the legal conclusion, while correct, does not address why a young person reached the point of suicide. This was the one dimension where the bulletin was felt to be incomplete by consensus.

Semi-trusting participants (those who initially believed 70-80% of the bulletin) increased their trust to 90% through exposure to the specific, verifiable data. The exact transfer counts (317, 179), specific amounts, and shared-account details were repeatedly cited as reasons for increased confidence.

consensus_across_panels.consensus: The police conclusion that Tan did not commit fraud is legally sound. The four-element test for fraud was never successfully challenged. The bidirectional financial data, shared savings accounts, and family meetings establish genuine romantic relationship beyond reasonable doubt.
consensus_across_panels.residual_concern: Even participants who accepted the legal conclusion expressed discomfort with the economic power imbalance in the relationship. This was channeled constructively into a three-layer consensus framework: legal (sound), psychological (deserves attention), social (systemic issues worth addressing).

consensus_across_panels.consensus: Liu's organized cyberbullying campaign was illegal. Grief does not justify paying ghostwriters, purchasing traffic, and deliberately causing someone to be mass-harassed online.
consensus_across_panels.residual_concern: Participants expressed empathy for Liu's grief and identified the need for better psychological support systems for trauma victims' families as a preventive measure against future vigilantism.

consensus_across_panels.consensus: The bulletin is factually credible. Its specific numerical data is verifiable and was not successfully challenged by any participant, including the designated skeptic.
consensus_across_panels.consensus_strength: STRONG - 6/6 agreed by round 5 (skeptic conceded factual accuracy by round 3)
consensus_across_panels.residual_concern: Public trust in official communications is a systemic issue that pre-dates this bulletin. Even accurate bulletins face trust deficits that can only be addressed through consistent transparency over time.

consensus_across_panels.consensus: The case should be understood primarily as a personal mental health tragedy, not as evidence for gender-based grievances. The gender-war framing distorts the facts and dishonors the deceased.
consensus_across_panels.consensus_strength: MODERATE-STRONG - 5/6 agreed by round 5; 6/6 by round 6
consensus_across_panels.residual_concern: The case does legitimately raise the issue of men's mental health support, but this concern should be addressed directly rather than weaponized in gender warfare.

identified_risks.risk: Selective citation of the 79.9M figure in unstructured environments
identified_risks.mitigation: Any public communication about this case should lead with the COMPLETE financial picture, not just the headline transfer figure

identified_risks.risk: Continued gender-war exploitation of the case
identified_risks.mitigation: Emphasize the panel finding that both gender-war sides distort the case; the issue is human (mental health), not gendered

identified_risks.risk: Trust deficit for future official bulletins in similar cases
identified_risks.mitigation: The panel demonstrated that specificity and verifiability earn trust. Future bulletins should maintain or increase data granularity.

constructive_outcomes: Identification of youth mental health support as the primary actionable takeaway from the case
Demonstration that structured, evidence-based discussion can overcome emotional distortion and ideological manipulation
Validation that official bulletins with specific, verifiable data can earn public trust even in highly charged environments
Identification of psychological aid for trauma victims' families as a preventive measure against cyberbullying vigilantism
Evidence that people with different starting positions can reach consensus through patient, multi-round dialogue
